title: When Independent Gaussian Models Break Down: Characterizing Regime-Dependent Modeling Failures in $φ^4$ Theory
authors: Anish Bhat, Ryo Ide, Zihan Zhao
abstract: In practical physical systems, modeling assumptions of Gaussianity and basis independence break down due to self-interactions. We study a specific instance of one-dimensional $φ^4$ theory on a lattice, analyzing how the interaction strength and system size jointly affect the marginal and joint distributions of frequency-based representation of the field (i.e., Fourier modes). We find that models relying on Gaussian and independent Fourier modes fail primarily from structured dependencies rather than marginal non-Gaussianity, since individual modes become approximately Gaussian despite mode coupling growing with size. Based on this, we identify three distinct regimes that delineate where traditional methods remain effective and where more expressive models are needed. Our results provide a computationally simple diagnostic to establish when Gaussian models are insufficient, and establish a concrete design criterion that future nonlinear models must satisfy.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the failure of Gaussian models in interacting field theories is primarily due to complex dependencies between modes rather than the non-Gaussian nature of individual modes. By identifying specific regimes of coupling, it provides a diagnostic tool to determine when more expressive, nonlinear models are necessary.
main_problem: The study investigates why Gaussian and independent Fourier-mode models fail to capture the complexity of self-interacting physical systems, specifically distinguishing between failures due to marginal non-Gaussianity versus structured inter-mode dependencies.
main_result: The authors find a separation between marginal simplicity and joint complexity: while individual Fourier modes become approximately Gaussian as system size increases, the inter-mode coupling grows, driving the failure of Gaussian models.
method: The researchers used Metropolis-Hastings MCMC to sample the 1D phi-4 theory and employed various diagnostics including a 4th-order coupling metric, spectral error, and KL divergence to compare Gaussian baselines against the true distribution.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional phi-4 theory on a lattice with periodic boundary conditions, defined by a Hamiltonian containing kinetic, mass, and quartic interaction terms.
key_observables: Two-point correlation function, power spectrum, normalized coupling metric (C), spectral error (epsilon), excess kurtosis, and Kullback-Leibler divergence.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (lambda), system size (N), and bare mass (m^2).
main_assumptions: The study is restricted to 1D lattice systems with periodic boundary conditions and focuses on 4th-order statistics, potentially overlooking higher-order cumulants.
figures_summary: The notes suggest figures demonstrating the scaling of Fourier mode Gaussianity (decreasing kurtosis) alongside the simultaneous growth of the coupling metric (C) and spectral error.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of modeling failures, describes the 1D phi-4 system and MCMC methodology, presents the diagnostic metrics, details the discovery of the separation between marginal and joint complexity, identifies three distinct coupling regimes, and concludes with implications for future nonlinear model design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: 1a: Empirically, for λ = 0.1, Kposition ≈−0.23, indicating near-Gaussian behavior. Largely independent of N, it saturates at −0.77, near the theoretical limit of −0.81 given by standard ϕ4 theory. 1b: Empirically, DKL, Fourier gradually decreases to DKL, Fourier ≈0.4. Similarly, KFourier approaches ≈0.14 with N, likewise indicating near-Gaussian marginals. Note that curves are mostly overlapped with one another.

Figure 2: Spectral relative error vs. normalized coupling across all baselines. Similar trends were observed for spectral error with respect to system size N, which is consistent with our observation that coupling increased with N, as across all λ, C ranges from C(N = 32) ≈0.06 to C(N = 128) ≈0.2.
